displayTitle: Adequate Light Levels and Glare Control

beats.narration: Most missed indications aren't failures of eyesight — they're failures of lighting. Let's give your eye a fair chance.

payload.title: LIGHT THE SURFACE
payload.sub: How much light you need, how to prove it, and how glare takes it away.

beats.narration: Two numbers rule the job: 500 lux for general examination, 1,000 lux for critical examination — both measured at the surface.
Prove it with a calibrated meter lying at the examination surface — working lamp, working distance, darkest spot. Then record the reading.

payload.heading: Prove the lux at the surface
payload.note: METER AT THE SURFACE · RECORD THE READING

beats.narration: Glare steals contrast three ways: direct — a bright source in view; reflected — light bouncing off shiny surfaces; veiling — scatter inside your own eye.
Know the minimum, prove it at the surface, and fix glare by moving — not by adding watts. Light's a tool. Set it up like one.

payload.sub: Light is a tool — set it up like one.
